content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES Corir’lIssIoN
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
August 30, 1999
Joel D. Kuperberg
Rutan & Tucker, LLP
611 Anton Boulevard, Suite 1400
Costa Mesa, California 92626-1998
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-223
Dear Mr. Kuperberg:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Irvine Mayor Christine
Shea regarding the conflict of interest provisions ofthe Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTIONS
1. Does Mayor Shea have an economic interest in a car wash company owned by her
former spouse’s family?
2. Does she have an economic interest in a trust established for her children by their
paternal grandfather?
3. Does she have an economic interest in the paternal grandfather’s estate from which her
children received an inheritance?
-
4. Does she have an economic interest in her former spouse from whom she has received
child support and presently receives alimony?
5. Does she have an economic interest in Tosco, her former spouse’s current employer,
on the basis that the alimony payments she receives most likely derive from his earnings with
that company?
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission rgu1ations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-223
Page 2
CONCLUSION
1-5. No, Mayor Shea does not have an economic interest in such persons or entities, as
described below, for purposes of the conflict of interest provisions of the Act.
FACTS
The frvine Hand Car Wash (“IIICW”) operates a car wash facility in the City of Irvine,
and also dispenses Shell gasoline. IHCW has applied for permits and land use entitlements to
change the signage for this facility, and to expand the facility to include a “lube shop.” Any
decision on these entitlements may be appealed to the city council.
Mayor Shea was previously married to an individual whose family owns a chain of car
washes (“Beacon Bay Car Wash Co.”), unrelated to IHCW. Mayor Shea was divorced in 1993.
Shortly afier the divorce, the mayor sold her community property interest in Beacon Bay Car
Wash Co. and received a one-time lump sum payment.
Mayor Shea has two children, one aged 19 and the other 21. The children’s paternal
grandfather, who was a principle in Beacon Bay Car Wash Co., established an education trust for
his grandchildren, including the mayor’s two children. The mayor believes that this trust will
disburse funds to her children, but she does not serve as a trustee or beneficiary of, or otherwise
have any control over, this trust. Upon the death oftheir grandfather, Mayor Shea’s children
received monies from his estate. Neither Mayor Shea nor her ex-husband was a beneficiary or
otherwise received anything from the estate.
The mayor’s former spouse now works for Tosco, which owns or otherwise has a
business relationship with Unocal, a competitor of Shell Oil Company. Following the divorce,
the mayor’s fonner spouse paid child support in the approximate amount of $2,200 per month for
approximately four years, until the youngest child turned 18. At that time, her former spouse
commenced alimony payments in the amount of approximately $749 per m nth, and Mayor Shea
continues to receive these payments. The child support and alimony payments most likely derive
from his salary received from Tosco. However, his obligation to make such payments is not
contingent upon his employment with Tosco or any other company.
ANALYSIS
The conflict of interest provisions ofthe Act prohibit public officials from making,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to use their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they have a financial interest. (Section 87100.) As the mayor of
Irvine, Ms. Shea is a public official for purposes of the Act. (Section 82048.)


File No. A-99-223
Page 3
Identifying Economic Interests
An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official, or on the following enumerated economic interests:
•
Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $1,000
or more.
•
Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or
more.
•
Any source of income of $250 or more provided to the official within 12 months before
the decision.
*
•
Any business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position ofmanagement.
•
Any donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the official within 12 months before
the decision. (Section 87103.)
A decision will have a financial effect “on the official” ifthe decision affect’s his or her
personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those ofhis or her immediate family.
(Section 87103.) This is known as the “personal financial effects” rule. (Regulation 18703.5).
1. Beacon Bay Car Wash Co.
Shortly after her divorce in 1993, Mayor Shea sold her ownership interest in Beacon Bay
Car Wash Co. to the company for a one-time lump sum. Thus, she no longer has an investment
interest in that company. (Section 87103(a).) Presumably, this transaction did not take place
within the last 12 months. Thus, the company is no longer a potentially disqualifying source of
income to her. (Section 87 103(c).) Under these facts, Mayor Shea does not have an economic
interest in Beacon Bay Car Wash Co.
2. The Trust
Mayor Shea’s children are beneficiaries of an education trust.
As set forth in the last paragraph of Section 87103, an official has an “indirect” economic
interest in an investment or real property interest held by his or her dependent child. An “interest
in real property” includes the pro rata share of interests inreal property held by a trust in which a
member ofthe official’s immediate family owns a 10 percent interest or greater. (Section


File No. A-99-223
Page 4
82033.) Similarly, the term “investment” includes a pro rata share of investments held by a trust
in which a member of the official’s immediate family owns a 10 percent interest or greater.
(Section 82034.)
The term “immediate family” means the spouse and dependent children. (Section
82029.) The Act does not define “dependent child.” However, we have advised that a dependent
child is under 18 years of age, and a dependent for federal income tax purposes. (Tremlett
Advice Letter, No. 1-89-386.)
Both of Mayor Shea’s children are over the age of 18. Consequently, whatever economic
interests they have with regard to the education trust are not economic interests of the mayor for
purposes of the Act.
3. The Inheritance
Upon the death of their grandfather, Mayor Shea’s children received monies from his
estate. Under the Act, only investments and real property interests held by a dependent child are
attributable to the official. The definition of “income” does not include income received by the
official’s child, even if the child is under 18 and entitled to be claimed as a dependent. (Section
82030(a).) Moreover, the term “income” excludes any devise or inheritance. (Section
82030(b)(3).) Thus, Mayor Shea does not have an economic interest in the paternal
grandfather’s estate.
4. Child Support and Alimony Payments
Following the divorce, Mayor Shea’s former spouse paid child support to her for four
years until the youngest. child was 18 years old. He currently pays her alimony. Presumably, the
mayor’s former spouse did not make a child support payment in the last 12 months. (Section
87103(c).) Moreover, the term “income” excludes alimony and child support payments.
(Section 82030(b)(7).) Therefore, Mayor Shea does not have an economic interest in her former
spouse as a source of income.
5. Tosco
The alimony payments received by the mayor most likely derive from her former
spouse’s salary from Tosco. Generally, the person who controls a payment made to an official is
considered to be the source of that income. (King Advice Letter, No. 1-99-145; Lucas Advice
Letter, No. A-99-059; Doyle Advice Letter, No. A-97-071.) Tosco has no control over alimony
payments made by Mayor Shea’s former spouse. Moreover, the term “income” excludes
alimony. (Section 82030(b)(7).) As such, the mayor does not have an economic interest in
Tosco as a source of income.


File No. A-99-223
Page 5
6. Personal Financial Effect
In addition to the economic interests listed above, a public official also has an economic
interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities. (Section 87103; Regulation
18703.5.) This rule applies irrespective of the source ofincome. (Underwood Advice Letter,
No. A-96-234.)
The alimony payments received by the mayor most likely derive from her forme’r
spouse’s earnings as an employee of Tosco. However, his obligation to make such payments is
not contingent upon his employment with Tosco or any other company.
Accordingly, it does
not appear that a decision affecting Tosco will have any affect on the mayor’s personal income
resulting from the alimony payments.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:JB:tls
